Lemma Quickstart
Docs GitHub lemma.work ↗
● Open source · AI-native workspace

Get started with Lemma

Pick the path that fits you. Answer one or two questions and we'll show you the single, simplest way to go from zero to a working pod.

1  Who are you 2  What you've got 3  Your exact steps
1

Are you a developer?

2

What do you want to set up?

2

Which AI coding agent do you have?

Lemma runs your agents on your AI — so you're not spending Lemma credits. Pick what you already pay for, or get a free one.

Developer path · Local stack Recommended for Mac users · local server → local CLI → build with your agent

Run Lemma locally

Use this when you want the full Lemma stack running on your own machine. Windows users should use WSL for the local stack.

★

Like what we've built? Star the repo

If Lemma looks useful to you, a star genuinely helps other builders find it. We're shipping fixes and new features constantly — starring is the easiest way to keep the repo on your radar and catch the latest updates. Want to contribute? Fork the repo and send a PR.

★ Star lemma-platform on GitHub ↗

1

Run the full stack locally

One command brings up everything in containers (needs Docker or Podman — the installer can set up Podman for you).

bash
curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/lemma-work/lemma-platform/main/install.sh | bash

When it's up, open http://127-0-0-1.sslip.io:3711 and create your account. (Use that host, not localhost — sign-in cookies are scoped to it.)

Windows: run the local stack inside WSL.

2

Install the CLI and point it at your local stack

bash
uv tool install lemma-terminal
lemma servers select local
lemma auth login

Which server? lemma servers select local points the CLI at the local stack you just started — that's what you build against on your machine. (To work against the hosted cloud instead, you'd use lemma servers cloud --use.)

3

Install Lemma skills

Add Lemma's pod-building skills to Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, or any supported coding agent so it knows how to build with the CLI.

bash
lemma skills install

If it ever errors on symlinks, it's safe to skip. The CLI is already installed and pointed at your local stack from the step above.

4

Use it from your own Claude Code or Codex

If you've done the steps above, you can start building directly from your IDE. Claude Code, Codex, or Cursor can use the installed Lemma skills automatically, so just ask your coding agent to build the pod, tables, agents, workflows, and apps you want with the lemma CLI.

No extra setup is needed here — keep building in your IDE and let your coding agent drive Lemma from the local CLI.

5

Create your first pod

A pod is one self-contained workspace — tables, agents, workflows, and apps for one job.

If you're using Claude Code, Codex, or another coding agent, you do not need to run this manually — you can continue building in your IDE and let the agent create the pod with the Lemma CLI.

bash
lemma pod create my-team --with-starter
lemma chat "what can you do in this pod?"
6

Deploy it to Lemma (hosted)

Want your pod reachable by teammates and surfaces without self-hosting? Push it to lemma.work. Export your local pod, point the CLI at the cloud, create a matching pod there, and import it in:

my-team below is the pod name from step 5 — swap in whatever you named yours.

bash
lemma pod export ./my-team
lemma servers cloud --use
lemma auth login
lemma pod create my-team
lemma pod import ./my-team

Built an app for the pod? Deploy that too (a no-build HTML file or a Vite project dir):

bash
lemma apps deploy my-app ./index.html
Credits: your lemma.work account comes with free starter credits — enough to deploy your pod and run it live on Lemma's hosted models.
That's it. You have Lemma running locally, a working pod, your own agent wired in, and a path to the cloud when you're ready.
Developer path · IDE + Lemma Cloud Recommended for Mac users · cloud CLI → skills → build from your IDE

Use Lemma from your IDE

Use this when you want Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, or another coding agent to build on hosted Lemma Cloud without running Lemma locally.

★

Like what we've built? Star the repo

If Lemma looks useful to you, a star genuinely helps other builders find it. We're shipping fixes and new features constantly — starring is the easiest way to keep the repo on your radar and catch the latest updates.

★ Star lemma-platform on GitHub ↗

1

Install the CLI and use Lemma Cloud

Install the Lemma CLI, point it at the hosted cloud server, and sign in with your Lemma account.

bash
uv tool install lemma-terminal
lemma servers cloud --use
lemma auth login

Which server? lemma servers cloud --use tells the CLI to build against hosted lemma.work, not a local stack.

2

Install Lemma skills

Add Lemma's pod-building skills to your coding agent so it knows how to build pods, tables, agents, workflows, and apps with the CLI.

bash
lemma skills install

If it ever errors on symlinks, it's safe to skip. The CLI is already installed and pointed at Lemma Cloud from the step above.

3

Open your IDE or coding agent

Open Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, or your preferred coding agent in the folder where you want to work. Ask it to build your Lemma pod or app using the installed Lemma skills and CLI.

You do not need to clone or run the Lemma platform repo for this path. Your coding agent will use your hosted Lemma account.

4

Create or deploy your first pod

You can let your coding agent drive this, or run a quick CLI smoke test yourself:

bash
lemma pod create my-team --with-starter
lemma chat "what can you do in this pod?"

Because the CLI is using Lemma Cloud, anything you create here is hosted on lemma.work.

That's it. Your IDE is set up to build on Lemma Cloud without running the local stack.
Developer path · Bring your own key Recommended for Windows and Mac · pod settings → runtime key → new conversation

Use your own API key on lemma.work

Recommended for both Windows and Mac users. Add your own model provider key to a hosted Lemma pod, then choose those models in new conversations. We currently support OpenAI-compatible and Anthropic-compatible keys.

1

Open a pod you have built

From lemma.work, open the pod where you want to use your own key. In the pod sidebar, click Settings.

2

Change the default agent runtime

In Pod Settings, stay on the General tab. Under Default Agent Runtime, click Change.

3

Choose a compatible key type

For OpenRouter or other OpenAI-compatible providers, choose OpenAI-compatible. For Anthropic-compatible providers, choose Anthropic-compatible.

Lemma currently supports only OpenAI-compatible and Anthropic-compatible keys.

4

Save your key details

Enter a name, base URL, API key, model IDs, and default model, then click Save.

5

Pick the model in a new conversation

Go to New conversation, open the model selector near the message box, choose the key/runtime you just added, pick an available model, and send your first message.

Temporary default-model bug: the default model may not update immediately after you save. This will be fixed tomorrow, June 27, 2026. For now, refresh the Settings page and set the default model family again. Your key is saved, and you can still use it by selecting the model manually in a new conversation.
Bring your own AI Paste one prompt → your agent builds & deploys an app

Use Lemma right inside your coding agent

Have Claude Code, Codex, or OpenCode? Paste one prompt and your coding agent installs Lemma, then builds and deploys a small app for you — you barely touch the Lemma website. About 5 minutes. (Your "coding agent" just means that coding app.)

🟢 First: get a free coding agent with OpenCode $0▾

No Claude Code or Codex subscription? Get a free coding agent with the OpenCode desktop app and OpenCode Zen free models — no terminal, and no card for the free tier. Here's the whole flow:

  1. Download the desktop app at opencode.ai/download.
  2. Open OpenCode Zen — go to opencode.ai/zen and sign in.
  3. Copy your API key from the Zen dashboard.
  4. In the app, open Connect provider and pick OpenCode Zen.
  5. Paste your key and hit Continue.
  6. Pick a free model — e.g. DeepSeek V4 Flash Free or Big Pickle — and you're ready.

Free models you can pick

  • Big Pickle — stealth model tuned for coding agents
  • DeepSeek V4 Flash Free — fast, capable, great for scaffolding
  • North Mini Code Free · MiMo-V2.5 Free · Nemotron 3 Ultra Free
Heads up: OpenCode's free models are free "for a limited time" — grab them now. Docs & pricing: opencode.ai/docs/zen
1

Create your Lemma account

Go to lemma.work, click Get started, and complete your sign-up or sign-in. New accounts get free starter credits automatically.

2

Open your coding agent

Open Claude Code, Codex, or OpenCode in any folder — a fresh folder or an existing project. That's where you'll work; you won't need the Lemma website again until your app is built.

3

Paste the setup prompt

Paste the prompt below into your coding agent. It installs the Lemma CLI and skills, points the CLI at Lemma Cloud, signs you in if needed, explains Lemma briefly, suggests a couple of tiny starter apps, then builds and deploys one. Just follow along and approve when it asks.

📋 Paste into your agent

      

      
Important: when your coding agent asks for permission to run install or CLI commands, allow it. It might ask for permissions for different commands multiple times — allow every time it asks so it can complete the setup.

      
    


    

      4
      
Sign in to Lemma

      
Your coding agent opens this page automatically in your browser. Sign in with the same account you used for your Lemma account — or if you're already signed in, it just uses that. Allow any browser permission it asks for, then return to your agent and tell it you're done.

      
    


    

      5
      
Installation completed

      
Come back to your coding agent — the setup is finished, and you can now just talk to it to build things. It may suggest a few small sample apps to show what's possible, or you can describe your own idea.

      
    


    

      6
      
Prompt your agent to build AI apps

      
Tell your coding agent which idea to build (or pick one of its suggestions). It gets to work on your pod — creating the tables, agents, and app, then deploying it — all using the Lemma skills and CLI. Just approve when it asks, and it hands you a link to your finished app. 🎉

      
    


    

      🛟 If something doesn't work▾
      

        
    
          
  • uv not found after install → run uv tool update-shell, or just open a fresh terminal, and continue.
    • 
          
  • It asks you to sign in again → just complete the browser sign-in once; it won't make you repeat it.
    • 
          
  • It lists several organizations → tell the agent which number is yours (usually your personal org).
    • 
          
  • An app needs Gmail / Slack → the agent pauses for you to approve the connection. Approve it, then tell the agent to continue.
    • 
          
  • Stuck anywhere → paste the error back to your coding agent; it can usually fix it on the spot.
    • 
        

      

    


    
You're live. You built and deployed a Lemma app without leaving your coding agent. Keep going: ask it to add a table, an agent, or a Gmail / WhatsApp channel.

  







  

    Lemma
    
The open-source workspace where humans and AI agents work as one team.
